name: capstone
documentation:
To install Capstone, you should run `pip install capstone`.

If you would like to build Capstone with just the source distribution, without
pip, just run `python setup.py install` in the folder with setup.py in it.

In order to use this source distribution, you will need an environment that can
compile C code. On Linux, this is usually easy, but on Windows, this involves
installing Visual Studio and using the "Developer Command Prompt" to perform the
installation. See BUILDING.txt for more information.

By default, attempting to install the python bindings will trigger a build of
the capstone native core. If this is undesirable for whatever reason, for
instance, you already have a globally installed copy of libcapstone, you may
inhibit the build by setting the environment variable LIBCAPSTONE_PATH. The
exact value is not checked, just setting it will inhibit the build. During
execution, this variable may be set to the path of a directory containing a
specific version of libcapstone you would like to use.

If you don't want to build your own copy of Capstone, you can use a precompiled
binary distribution from PyPI. Saying `pip install capstone` should
automatically obtain an appropriate copy for your system. If it does not, please
open an issue at https://github.com/aquynh/capstone and tag @rhelmot - she
will fix this, probably!

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Capstone is a disassembly framework with the target of becoming the ultimate
disasm engine for binary analysis and reversing in the security community.

Created by Nguyen Anh Quynh, then developed and maintained by a small community,
Capstone offers some unparalleled features:

- Support multiple hardware architectures: ARM, ARM64 (ARMv8), Mips, PPC, Sparc,
  SystemZ, XCore and X86 (including X86_64).

- Having clean/simple/lightweight/intuitive architecture-neutral API.

- Provide details on disassembled instruction (called “decomposer” by others).

- Provide semantics of the disassembled instruction, such as list of implicit
  registers read & written.

- Implemented in pure C language, with lightweight wrappers for C++, C#, Go,
  Java, NodeJS, Ocaml, Python, Ruby & Vala ready (available in main code,
  or provided externally by the community).

- Native support for all popular platforms: Windows, Mac OSX, iOS, Android,
  Linux, *BSD, Solaris, etc.

- Thread-safe by design.

- Special support for embedding into firmware or OS kernel.

- High performance & suitable for malware analysis (capable of handling various
  X86 malware tricks).

- Distributed under the open source BSD license.

Further information is available at http://www.capstone-engine.org


[License]

This project is released under the BSD license. If you redistribute the binary
or source code of Capstone, please attach file LICENSE.TXT with your products.

functions.name: capstone.copy_ctypes
documentation: Returns a new ctypes object which is a bitwise copy of an existing one

functions.name: capstone.cs_disasm_quick
documentation: Quick & dirty Python function to disasm raw binary code
NOTE: you might want to use more efficient Cs class & its methods.

functions.name: capstone.cs_disasm_lite
documentation: Another quick, but lighter function to disasm raw binary code.
This function is faster than cs_disasm_quick() around 20% because
cs_disasm_lite() only return tuples of (address, size, mnemonic, op_str),
NOTE: you might want to use more efficient Cs class & its methods.

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_DETAIL
documentation: Break down instruction structure into details

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_SKIPDATA_SETUP
documentation: Setup user-defined function for SKIPDATA option

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_NO_BRANCH_OFFSET
documentation: ARM, prints branch immediates without offset.

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_OFF
documentation: Turn OFF an option - default option of CS_OPT_DETAIL

variables.name: capstone.CS_OP_MEM
documentation: Memory operand. Can be ORed with another operand type.

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_SYNTAX_DEFAULT
documentation: Default assembly syntax of all platforms (CS_OPT_SYNTAX)

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_SYNTAX_INTEL
documentation: Intel X86 asm syntax - default syntax on X86 (CS_OPT_SYNTAX, CS_ARCH_X86)

variables.name: capstone.CS_OPT_SYNTAX_NOREGNAME
documentation: Asm syntax prints register name with only number - (CS_OPT_SYNTAX, CS_ARCH_PPC, CS_ARCH_ARM)

variables.name: capstone.CS_ERR_CSH
documentation: Invalid csh argument: cs_close(), cs_errno(), cs_option()

variables.name: capstone.CS_ERR_SKIPDATA
documentation: Access irrelevant data for "data" instruction in SKIPDATA mode

variables.name: capstone.CS_ERR_X86_ATT
documentation: X86 AT&T syntax is unsupported (opt-out at compile time)

variables.name: capstone.CS_ERR_X86_INTEL
documentation: X86 Intel syntax is unsupported (opt-out at compile time)

variables.name: capstone.CS_ERR_X86_MASM
documentation: X86 Intel syntax is unsupported (opt-out at compile time)

classes.name: capstone.CsError
documentation: access to error code via @errno of CsError

classes.name: capstone._dummy_cs
documentation: dummy class resembling Cs class, just for cs_disasm_quick()
this class only need to be referenced to via 2 fields: @csh & @arch

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.bytes
documentation: return instruction's machine bytes (which should have @size bytes).

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.regs_read
documentation: return list of all implicit registers being read.

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.regs_write
documentation: return list of all implicit registers being modified

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.groups
documentation: return list of semantic groups this instruction belongs to.

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.reg_name
documentation: get the register name, given the register ID

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.group
documentation: verify if this insn belong to group with id as @group_id

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.reg_read
documentation: verify if this instruction implicitly read register @reg_id

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.reg_write
documentation: verify if this instruction implicitly modified register @reg_id

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.op_count
documentation: return number of operands having same operand type @op_type

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.op_find
documentation: get the operand at position @position of all operands having the same type @op_type

methods.name: capstone.CsInsn.regs_access
documentation: This includes all the implicit & explicit registers.

methods.name: capstone.Cs.__del__
documentation: destructor to be called automatically when object is destroyed.

methods.name: capstone.Cs.x86_reduce
documentation: is this engine compiled with X86-reduce option?

methods.name: capstone.Cs.support
documentation: check to see if this engine supports a particular arch,

methods.name: capstone.Cs.mode
documentation: return disassembly mode of this engine.

methods.name: capstone.Cs.reg_name
documentation: get the register name, given the register ID

methods.name: capstone.Cs.insn_name
documentation: get the instruction name, given the instruction ID

methods.name: capstone.Cs.disasm
documentation: Disassemble binary & return disassembled instructions in CsInsn objects

methods.name: capstone.Cs.disasm_lite
documentation: Light function to disassemble binary. This is about 20% faster than disasm() because
unlike disasm(), disasm_lite() only return tuples of (address, size, mnemonic, op_str),

functions.name: setup.copy_sources
documentation:
Copy the C sources into the source directory.
This rearranges the source files under the python distribution
directory.

functions.name: setup.build_libraries
documentation:
Prepare the capstone directory for a binary distribution or installation.
Builds shared libraries and copies header files.

Will use a src/ dir if one exists in the current directory, otherwise assumes it's in the repo
